summary: I was about 14 or 15 when Pearl Harbor happened, right around my birthday. That day changed everything; suddenly people treated us differently, even teachers called us 'Jap boy,' which really hurt. My parents seemed to have a feeling that war was coming, though not exactly at Pearl Harbor. It was a shocking event that marked the start of a difficult time for us.

I lived near Japantown in San Jose, but it wasn’t a very large Japanese community. The area was mostly rural and beautiful before many cars came in. People from different parts of Japan, like Kumamoto and Fukuoka Prefectures, lived there and tended to stick together. It was a small town atmosphere, and my family was part of that close-knit community.

My family mostly came from Kumamoto Prefecture, and many others in our community did too. My father had friends from Kumamoto who used to come over, and I learned a lot from listening to their conversations. Even during the war, people from Kumamoto tended to stick together. It was part of our identity and how we connected with others in San Jose.

There were also people from Fukuoka Prefecture living in our area near San Jose. Like those from Kumamoto, they formed part of the Japanese community here. My folks mostly kept to their own groups, so Fukuoka folks were part of that network. It was a way to maintain some cultural ties during those uncertain times.

When Executive Order 9066 was issued, we had to leave San Jose and go to an assembly center. It was a sudden and harsh order that uprooted our lives. My family was sent to the Santa Anita Assembly Center before being moved to Heart Mountain. It was a difficult time, but my mother insisted we stay together as a family despite the challenges.

I grew up in San Jose, California, in a mostly rural area near Japantown. It was a small town with a close-knit Japanese community, many from Kumamoto and Fukuoka Prefectures. Life was simple and beautiful before the war, but after Pearl Harbor and the Executive Order 9066, everything changed. We were forced to leave San Jose and sent to camps, which was heartbreaking.

After leaving San Jose due to Executive Order 9066, we were sent to the Santa Anita Assembly Center, where we stayed in barracks, though some were put in the stables. Despite the harsh conditions, we tried to make the best of it and had some fun. I remember a popular bowler named Fuzzy Shimada who was known for sleeping a lot and got put in the worst spot as a joke. It was a strange and difficult place, but it was only temporary before we moved on to Heart Mountain.

Fuzzy Shimada was a well-known bowler at the Santa Anita Assembly Center and a funny character because he was always sleeping. He used to sleep with a group of athlete boys, and as a joke, someone pushed him into the worst spot where everyone walked by. I knew him and the guy who did it, and it was a light moment amid tough times. Fuzzy was a good sleeper and a memorable person from that period.

After three months at Santa Anita, we were sent to Heart Mountain Relocation Center in Wyoming. When we arrived, it was dusty and bleak, and I remember feeling like an angel thrown into a harsh place. There were armed young soldiers guarding us, and though they didn’t mean harm, the fences and towers made it clear we were prisoners. I was about 16 and started school there, meeting new friends but also facing tension with people from other camps like L.A.

During the train trip from Santa Anita to Heart Mountain, I remember seeing a brightly lit place through the train curtain and guessing it was Reno, Nevada. I didn’t know much about Reno then, but it stood out as a bright spot on that long, rattling journey. The trip was tough, with kids crying and everyone feeling scared and uncertain. Reno was just a brief glimpse of normal life outside the confinement.

Frank Emi was a leader among us at Heart Mountain and known for speaking out during the loyalty questionnaire period. He was a judo teacher and someone many of us looked up to. When the loyalty questionnaire came out, he and my brother Kazuto were among the first to refuse to answer certain questions. Frank’s leadership helped shape our resistance and sense of identity during those difficult times.

Kazuto Emi was my older brother by about a year or so, and during the loyalty questionnaire at Heart Mountain, he was one of the first to refuse to answer the controversial questions. I followed his lead because I wasn’t sure what to do. He was strong in his convictions, and his stance influenced many of us. Kazuto’s courage during that time was important for our family and community.

Art Emi was my younger brother, and like the rest of us, he experienced the upheaval of the war and relocation. Though not much was said about him specifically, he was part of our large family that stayed together through the camps. Our parents worked hard to keep us all united, and Art was part of that close-knit family dynamic. We all shared the struggles and memories of those years.

The loyalty questionnaire at Heart Mountain was a tense and confusing time for all of us. Questions 27 and 28 were especially controversial, asking about our willingness to serve in the military and forswear allegiance to Japan. My brother Kazuto and Frank Emi were among the first to say no, and many others followed. It caused divisions and tough choices, but I mostly went along with my brother because I was unsure. It was a defining moment in our camp experience.
